Pick one cluster. Prove it against a measurable regulatory or commercial metric. Extend.

faq.question: Can AI decisions meet FCA explainability expectations?
faq.answer: Yes, when the model and its surround are designed for it. That means challenger models, per-decision reason codes, feature attribution, and human review where the stakes demand it. We design for a Skilled Person Review, not just a first audit.

faq.question: How do you handle Consumer Duty for AI-driven decisions?
faq.answer: Consumer Duty fair-value and foreseeable-harm obligations apply to AI outputs the same as any other decision. We build in outcome monitoring, vulnerability detection, and fair-value evidence as first-class artefacts — not post-hoc documents.

faq.question: Can you integrate with our core banking or ledger?
faq.answer: Yes. We have worked across ThoughtMachine Vault, Mambu, 10x, and bespoke ledger platforms. We design for eventual migration risk as well as current integration.

faq.question: What is the posture on data residency and cloud?
faq.answer: Default is UK data residency, production workloads inside your existing AWS, Azure or GCP estate, and private model endpoints rather than public APIs for any regulated workflow.

faq.question: Do you work with pre-authorisation fintechs?
faq.answer: Yes, though the engagement looks different — more focused on building the governance and evidence the FCA will expect to see at authorisation, with agent workloads kept narrow until you are live.

faq.question: How quickly does a fintech see results?
faq.answer: A focused pilot — a KYC agent or a transaction monitoring enhancement — typically ships in 8 to 12 weeks. Regulatory posture sign-off may add time depending on your internal model risk process.

pages.name: AI for Insurance
pages.title: AI Consultant for Insurance — Agentic AI Associates
pages.description: Agentic AI for UK insurers, MGAs and brokers. Claims triage, underwriting copilots, fraud detection, policy document intelligence, and regulator-ready governance.
pages.intro:
Insurance is a business of documents, decisions and distribution — and each of those three surfaces is where agentic AI changes the economics. Documents, because every claim, quote and policy is a piece of unstructured text that has historically needed a human. Decisions, because underwriting and claims handling are decision pipelines with clear audit requirements. Distribution, because broker and customer interactions are where retention is won or lost.

We work with UK insurers, MGAs and brokers across personal lines, commercial, specialty and Lloyd's market models. The technology stack differs — Guidewire, Duck Creek, bespoke policy admin, London Market platforms — but the operational patterns repeat. Claims queues grow faster than adjusters. Underwriting appetite and referral rules drift out of sync with the portfolio. And the FCA, Lloyd's and the PRA each want something slightly different from the same data.

Every engagement opens with an AI Readiness Assessment that maps your policy admin, claims, distribution and rating stack before anything is scoped.

painPoints.title: Claims cycle times stay stubbornly long
painPoints.description: FNOL, triage, allocation, adjuster workflow and settlement each add days. The customer experiences one timeline; the insurer sees five handoffs — each is an opportunity to lose trust.

painPoints.title: Underwriting referral rules drift
painPoints.description: As the book grows, referral rules that made sense at launch produce too many or too few referrals. Underwriters lose hours on trivial cases while real exposures slip through unreviewed.

painPoints.title: Fraud patterns outpace rule-based detection
painPoints.description: Organised fraud rings evolve faster than quarterly rule reviews. The cases that reach SIU are often the obvious ones; the expensive ones are already paid out.

painPoints.title: Broker and distribution data never reconciles
painPoints.description: Broker portals, bordereaux feeds, and core policy admin produce different numbers for the same portfolio. Commission disputes and regulatory reporting both suffer.

solutionSections.heading: Agentic Claims, FNOL and Underwriting Automation
solutionSections.body: We build agents that own specific insurance workflows end-to-end, with strict approval paths where regulation demands. A claims triage agent reads FNOL notifications, classifies severity and complexity, and routes to the right adjuster queue with a first-pass reserve recommendation. A document-intelligence agent extracts data from policy schedules, medical reports and engineers' reports into structured fields the core system can use. A pre-underwriting agent prepares a submission pack for a complex risk — pulling public data, previous loss history, and sanctions checks — before the underwriter sees it. See our agentic AI approach and automation work.

solutionSections.heading: AI-Powered Portfolio and Loss Analysis
solutionSections.body: Insurance has always been a data industry, but most carriers still analyse on cycles — monthly portfolio reviews, quarterly reserving, annual strategy. We build pipelines that shift that rhythm to daily or hourly: loss-ratio drift alerts by segment, frequency and severity anomalies on fresh claims, and emerging-risk signal from unstructured claims narratives. Our data AI patterns preserve the Solvency II and Lloyd's Minimum Standards posture you already operate under.

solutionSections.heading: FCA, PRA and Lloyd's Ready AI Governance
solutionSections.body: Insurance AI governance is not just FCA — it is FCA, PRA, Lloyd's Minimum Standards, Consumer Duty, fair-value assessments and, for pricing, the General Insurance Pricing Practices regime. Our AI governance framework is built for that matrix: model documentation, bias and fairness testing, walk-forward validation, and the audit pack your actuarial function and Skilled Person Review will expect.

solutionSections.heading: Reserving, Capital and Claims Forecasting
solutionSections.body: Reserving, capital modelling and claims-frequency forecasting all benefit from the same treatment: replace static, lagged models with pipelines that refresh against fresh data and stay explainable to the actuarial function. We build that infrastructure alongside your actuaries rather than around them — the goal is to compress the cycle, not replace the judgement. See how our applied AI forecasting patterns extend across regulated sectors.

solutionSections.heading: High-ROI AI Use Cases for UK Insurers
solutionSections.body:
Insurers tend to find the highest ROI in these five clusters. Each can ship as a pilot in 8-12 weeks.

  • Claims and FNOL: FNOL triage, severity classification, reserve recommendation, document extraction, adjuster copilot.
  • Underwriting and risk: submission enrichment, referral-rule optimisation, exposure concentration alerts, pre-bind checks.
  • Fraud and SIU: claims narrative analysis, network and link analysis, organised-fraud pattern detection, investigator copilot.
  • Customer and broker operations: first-line support, broker support automation, renewals and retention scoring, complaint triage.
  • Pricing and actuarial support: walk-forward pricing validation, GIPP evidence generation, reserving cycle compression, experience studies.

Pick one cluster. Ship it. Extend.

faq.question: Do you work with Lloyd's market syndicates and MGAs?
faq.answer: Yes. The Lloyd's posture is different — MGAA, coverholder audits, bordereaux reporting and Lloyd's Minimum Standards all shape what agents can and cannot do. We design with that reality in mind rather than discovering it late.

faq.question: How do you handle GIPP and Consumer Duty for AI-driven pricing?
faq.answer: GIPP compliance and Consumer Duty fair-value overlap — both need evidence of outcome testing and fair treatment across segments. We build monitoring for both as part of the AI pipeline, not as a retrospective report.

faq.question: What core systems do you integrate with?
faq.answer: Guidewire ClaimCenter/PolicyCenter, Duck Creek, Sapiens, and a long list of bespoke policy admin and Lloyd's platforms. Integration pattern depends on how much control you have of the data layer.

faq.question: Can agents operate inside a regulated underwriting decision?
faq.answer: Yes, provided the decision architecture is transparent — challenger-model validation, feature attribution, and human sign-off on anything that materially changes premium or declines cover. We design for auditability first, speed second.

faq.question: How do you handle sensitive claims data and data minimisation?
faq.answer: All processing defaults to inside your estate; sensitive categories (health, vulnerability) get additional controls by default; model inputs are minimised to what the decision actually needs. UK GDPR Article 22 posture is baked in.

faq.question: How quickly does an insurer see impact?
faq.answer: A focused pilot — FNOL triage or submission enrichment — usually ships in 8 to 12 weeks. Loss-ratio impact typically takes longer because of the reserving cycle, but operational metrics (cycle time, queue depth, referral quality) move in the first quarter.

pages.name: AI for Logistics & Fleet
pages.title: AI Consultant for Logistics & Fleet — Agentic AI Associates
pages.description: Agentic AI for UK logistics, last-mile and fleet operators. Route optimisation, driver ops, exception handling, telematics intelligence, and customer comms that stay accurate in real time.
pages.intro:
Logistics runs on two currencies: minutes and miles. Every operational decision — routing, loading, driver allocation, exception handling — is a trade between them. Agentic AI changes the decision cadence. Instead of a daily plan that degrades from the first missed pickup, you get a live system that replans as reality unfolds and keeps the customer communication in step with it.

We work with UK logistics businesses across 3PL, parcel, last-mile, specialist and own-fleet operators. The operational truth in every one of them is the same: the plan is obsolete the moment the first van leaves, and the cost of replanning manually is higher than the cost of the disruption.

Every engagement opens with an AI Readiness Assessment that maps your TMS, WMS, telematics and customer comms stack — because in logistics, the data is in five systems and none of them agrees with the others on where a parcel is.

painPoints.title: Plans decay the moment the day starts
painPoints.description: Route plans built overnight lose accuracy on the first traffic incident, failed delivery or late collection. Manual replanning lags reality by hours.

painPoints.title: Customer comms drift out of step with operations
painPoints.description: The tracking page says one thing, the driver knows another, and the contact centre gets the call. Most complaints are not about failure — they are about being told the wrong thing.

painPoints.title: Driver ops still run on paper and phone calls
painPoints.description: Exception handling, POD disputes, returns processing and driver check-in are often still manual. Each one is minutes added to the shift and a margin hit over a month.

painPoints.title: Telematics data is gathered, not used
painPoints.description: Most fleets pay for telematics and use 10% of what it produces. The patterns in fuel burn, harsh-braking, idling and route adherence rarely feed back into daily decisions.

solutionSections.heading: Agentic Dispatch, Exception Handling and Customer Comms
solutionSections.body: We build agents that live in the operational loop. A dispatch agent replans routes continuously as exceptions land, with clear guardrails on customer-commitment windows. An exception agent handles failed deliveries, returns triage, and driver escalations with templated playbooks and the right human sign-off points. A customer comms agent keeps the tracking page, ETA notifications and contact centre notes aligned from the same source of truth. See our agentic AI approach and conversational AI for logistics-grade applications.

solutionSections.heading: AI-Powered Telematics and Performance Analysis
solutionSections.body: Fleet telematics, WMS throughput, and driver performance data contain more than enough signal to drive daily decisions — most operators just never wire it together. We build pipelines that unify telematics, TMS, and WMS into per-driver, per-route, per-depot dashboards with anomaly detection on top. The output is not another report — it is a set of triggers the operations team actually acts on. Our data AI patterns are designed for the depot-level latency and connectivity realities of real logistics environments.

solutionSections.heading: Driver Hours, DVSA and Duty-of-Care Monitoring
solutionSections.body: Driver hours (WTD and RTD regs), DVSA operator licence conditions, tachograph compliance and duty-of-care obligations are non-negotiable and largely still monitored manually. Our AI governance and compliance work packages these into continuous monitoring — so an infringement risk is flagged in-shift, not discovered during an audit.

solutionSections.heading: Demand, Volume and Capacity Forecasting
solutionSections.body: Volume forecasting for logistics has to work at multiple horizons at once — next-hour capacity for a depot, next-week staffing plan, next-month peak prep. We build forecasting pipelines that produce coherent numbers across those horizons, trained on your own history rather than sector averages that miss your customer mix. See how our applied AI forecasting patterns translate across operational sectors.

solutionSections.heading: High-ROI AI Use Cases for UK Logistics Operators
solutionSections.body:
Logistics operators tend to compound value from these five clusters — each is a pilot in 8-12 weeks.

  • Dynamic routing and dispatch: live route replanning, stop sequencing, driver-vehicle matching, congestion-aware ETAs.
  • Exception and returns handling: failed-delivery triage, returns routing, driver escalation agents, POD dispute resolution.
  • Customer experience: live ETA accuracy, proactive delay comms, contact centre deflection, complaint triage.
  • Driver and fleet operations: driver coaching insights from telematics, fuel burn anomaly detection, hours-of-service monitoring.
  • Depot and warehouse: labour planning, putaway optimisation, dock scheduling, inventory anomaly detection.

Pick one. Ship a 90-day pilot. Extend into the adjacent cluster.

faq.question: Do you work with 3PLs as well as own-fleet operators?
faq.answer: Yes. The operational shape is different — 3PLs have customer SLAs and multi-shipper data isolation to manage — but the agent patterns port cleanly with the right design.

faq.question: Can AI replace our TMS or WMS?
faq.answer: Almost never, and we would rarely recommend it. The highest-value work tends to sit on top of existing TMS/WMS as orchestration and intelligence, not as a replacement. Replacement projects are two-year programmes; orchestration projects are two-quarter programmes.

faq.question: What telematics platforms do you support?
faq.answer: The common ones — Samsara, Lightfoot, Webfleet, MichelinConnectedFleet, Microlise, Quartix — plus direct integration with tachograph downloads and bespoke platforms. We build once and make the downstream models platform-agnostic.

faq.question: Will drivers accept AI in the cab?
faq.answer: Driver adoption is an operational problem, not a technology one. We design agent interventions to make the driver's shift easier — better sequencing, fewer admin taps, fewer useless calls from the contact centre — not to surveil. Adoption follows usefulness.

faq.question: How do you handle the connectivity gaps in real-world fleets?
faq.answer: Every design assumes intermittent connectivity. Edge caching, offline-first data capture, and deferred-sync reconciliation are baked in. You cannot run logistics AI as if every vehicle has a perfect 4G connection, because they never do.

faq.question: How quickly does a logistics operator see impact?
faq.answer: A focused pilot — a dispatch agent or a customer comms agent — typically ships in 8 to 12 weeks. Operational metrics (failed deliveries, contact centre volume, driver overtime) move inside the first quarter.

pages.name: AI for Energy & Utilities

pages.intro:
Energy and water utilities run two parallel businesses at once: a physical network business and a regulated consumer business. Each produces its own data — SCADA, smart meters, network telemetry, billing, CRM, field work orders — and almost none of it is integrated where a daily decision actually happens. Agentic AI is one of the few levers that can genuinely work across that divide.

We work with UK suppliers, DNOs, and water companies on the specific problems that regulation and net-zero obligations have made urgent: smart meter data that needs to drive tariffs in near-real-time, network assets with ageing profiles that cannot wait for a five-yearly review, and customer operations under Ofgem or Ofwat scrutiny.

Every engagement opens with an AI Readiness Assessment that maps your SCADA, metering, billing and field systems before anything is scoped.

painPoints.title: Smart meter data is gathered but not decisioned
painPoints.description: Half-hourly data is a regulatory asset most suppliers still treat as a billing input. The price-signalling, demand-response and churn-prediction value goes largely unrealised.

painPoints.title: Asset health runs on calendar, not condition
painPoints.description: Transformers, mains and substations get scheduled inspections rather than condition-based ones. The cost is either over-maintenance or the occasional preventable failure at regulatory cost.

painPoints.title: Customer operations buckle under tariff churn
painPoints.description: Price cap changes, tariff switches and TVB obligations generate volume that contact centres cannot staff through. NPS and complaint volumes move in the wrong direction simultaneously.

painPoints.title: Field work is planned separately from network reality
painPoints.description: Work order planning is often disconnected from live network condition and customer-impact modelling. The wrong jobs get prioritised, and the regulator notices.

solutionSections.heading: Agentic Customer Operations and Field Work Orchestration
solutionSections.body: We build agents that own specific utility workflows end-to-end with full audit trail. A customer-ops agent handles tariff switches, vulnerable-customer flags, and complaint triage with Ofgem Consumer Standards baked in. A field-work agent takes work orders, asset condition data, customer-impact modelling and crew availability and produces a daily plan that holds up against reality. A meter-read exceptions agent clears MPAN/MPRN data issues that currently sit in a team inbox for weeks. See our agentic AI approach.

solutionSections.heading: Smart Meter, SCADA and Asset Data Integration
solutionSections.body: The data is there — smart meter half-hourly reads, SCADA, GIS, asset registers, DNO flow data — it is just not integrated where decisions happen. We build pipelines that unify these feeds into per-asset, per-customer, per-circuit models with anomaly detection and forecasting on top. Our data AI patterns handle the real-world messiness — inconsistent meter read quality, GIS/asset register mismatches, SCADA latency — rather than assuming clean data.

solutionSections.heading: Ofgem, Ofwat and Consumer Standards Monitoring
solutionSections.body: Utilities operate under a regulator matrix that other sectors rarely match — Ofgem, Ofwat, the DCC framework, Consumer Duty analogues, vulnerable-customer obligations, and the price cap mechanics. solutionSections.heading: Demand, Asset Health and Price Exposure Forecasting
solutionSections.body: Utilities need three simultaneous forecasts: demand at half-hourly resolution, asset failure probability on a long horizon, and commercial price exposure on a trading horizon. We build forecasting pipelines that produce coherent numbers across those scales, trained on your own data and the weather/market signals that actually drive them. See how our applied AI forecasting extends across regulated sectors.

Utilities tend to get the most return from these five clusters.

faq.question: Do you work with suppliers as well as network operators?
faq.answer: Yes, though the operational shape differs significantly. Suppliers have retail customer dynamics; DNOs have network and safety regulation. We design the agent architecture to match — shared infrastructure, different guardrails.

faq.question: How long until a utility sees impact?

pages.name: AI for Pharma & Life Sciences
pages.title: AI Consultant for Pharma & Life Sciences — Agentic AI Associates
pages.description: Agentic AI for UK pharma, biotech and life sciences. Regulatory document intelligence, pharmacovigilance automation, clinical operations, and MHRA-ready governance.
pages.intro:
Pharma and life sciences live in a document economy. Every decision — a clinical trial protocol, a regulatory submission, a pharmacovigilance signal, a medical information response — is a piece of carefully written text that has historically needed a qualified human to read, interpret and respond. Agentic AI, used with discipline, can take a lot of the reading and drafting off the qualified human without taking the judgement.

We work with UK pharma, biotech and medical device companies on exactly the workflows where agents can compress cycle time without touching the regulated judgement call. That means document intelligence, case processing, and operational automation — not decisions that require a QP signature or a clinical author.

Every engagement opens with an AI Readiness Assessment that maps your regulatory operations, clinical operations, PV system, and medical information stack — because in pharma the system is the process.

painPoints.title: Regulatory operations are doc-heavy and bottlenecked
painPoints.description: Preparing a CTA, MAA or variation submission consumes regulatory writers, labelling specialists and publishing teams for months. Cycle time is the single biggest controllable cost of going to market.

painPoints.title: Pharmacovigilance case volume outpaces hiring
painPoints.description: ICSR volume grows with indications, geographies and digital channels. PV case processing costs scale close to linearly with volume; the economics will break for most mid-cap pharma before 2030.

painPoints.title: Medical information response times degrade
painPoints.description: MI queries from HCPs and patients must be answered accurately and on time. Scaling the medical information function without compromising quality is a recurring operational headache.

painPoints.title: Clinical ops data sits in silos
painPoints.description: EDC, CTMS, eTMF and safety systems each hold part of the clinical picture. Study teams spend more time reconciling systems than running the study.

solutionSections.heading: Agentic Document Intelligence and Case Processing
solutionSections.body: We build agents that own specific life-sciences workflows end-to-end, with the sign-off gates pharma demands. A regulatory document agent extracts and drafts sections of CTD modules, variations, and clinical study reports — with a medical writer reviewing and approving every output. A PV case intake agent handles ICSR triage, duplicate detection, MedDRA coding and narrative first-draft, with case processors reviewing before submission. A medical information agent drafts responses against the MI letter library with referenceable provenance. See our agentic AI approach.

solutionSections.heading: AI-Powered Clinical and Safety Data Analysis
solutionSections.body: Clinical and safety data analysis is a regulated analytics problem — the statistical rigour is non-negotiable, but the data preparation and signal-detection surround is where AI gives real lift. We build pipelines that accelerate signal detection in PV, cross-study safety monitoring, and protocol deviation analysis, all with the validation posture and 21 CFR Part 11 / EU Annex 11 alignment the MHRA expects. Our data AI patterns preserve the computerised-system-validation posture rather than route around it.

solutionSections.heading: MHRA, EMA and GxP-Aligned AI Governance
solutionSections.body: AI in a GxP environment is a qualified-system problem. Validation, change control, data integrity (ALCOA+), 21 CFR Part 11 / Annex 11 alignment, and the MHRA's evolving expectations for AI all land on the same validation plan. Our AI governance framework is built for that environment — IQ/OQ/PQ artefacts, change control discipline, and the audit trail an MHRA or EMA inspection will ask for.

solutionSections.heading: Demand, Supply and Enrolment Forecasting
solutionSections.body: Pharma forecasting problems repeat — trial enrolment projection, supply-chain risk forecasting against manufacturing capacity, launch-demand modelling. We build forecasting pipelines that layer onto your existing forecasting process rather than replace it, with explainability your finance and commercial teams can defend. Our applied AI forecasting work translates the same patterns across operational sectors.

solutionSections.heading: High-ROI AI Use Cases for UK Pharma and Biotech
solutionSections.body:
Pharma tends to compound value from these clusters. Each is a pilot in 8-12 weeks inside an appropriate validation wrapper.

  • Regulatory operations: CTD module drafting, labelling comparison, variation impact assessment, regulatory intelligence monitoring.
  • Pharmacovigilance: ICSR intake triage, MedDRA coding assistance, narrative drafting, literature signal detection.

Each cluster lives inside its own validation wrapper. No shortcut.

faq.question: How do you handle GxP validation for AI systems?
faq.answer: AI in GxP contexts is validated as a computerised system — risk-assessed, functionally specified, IQ/OQ/PQ evidenced, with change control and periodic review. We work to GAMP 5 alignment and the specific expectations the MHRA has published for AI in regulated contexts.

faq.question: Can AI operate inside a regulated decision?
faq.answer: In GxP contexts, agents typically draft and extract — qualified humans always approve. We design the approval gates and evidence trail so the qualified person's role is preserved, not eroded.

faq.question: What systems do you integrate with?
faq.answer: The regulated-system stack — Veeva Vault, ArisGlobal, Oracle Argus, Medidata Rave, IQVIA platforms, and the common eTMF and CTMS platforms — plus the MHRA submission channels and EudraVigilance.

faq.question: Can you work within our computerised system validation framework?
faq.answer: Yes. We expect to operate inside your CSV framework, not around it. Every deliverable fits the validation plan — user requirements, functional specs, test scripts, traceability — rather than being bolted on after the fact.

faq.question: How do you handle patient data and ALCOA+ principles?
faq.answer: All processing defaults to inside your validated estate, patient identifiers are minimised or tokenised, audit trails preserve attribution and contemporaneity, and every system is designed to the ALCOA+ principles from day one.

faq.question: How quickly does a pharma engagement show return?
faq.answer: Faster than most expect on ops metrics (cycle times, case throughput, MI response times) — 8 to 12 weeks on a focused pilot. Regulatory cycle-time improvements take longer because submission cycles are long.

Our AI governance framework packages this as continuous monitoring rather than annual scramble.

solutionSections.heading: Income, Demand and Capacity Forecasting for Charities
solutionSections.body: Charity forecasting has to reconcile three things at once: income by stream, demand on services, and staff and volunteer capacity. We build forecasting pipelines that produce coherent numbers across those — with explainability trustees can read. Our applied AI forecasting patterns translate across mission-led organisations.

solutionSections.heading: High-ROI AI Use Cases for UK Charities
solutionSections.body:
Charities tend to get the highest return from these clusters. Each is a pilot in 8-12 weeks.

  • Fundraising and supporter care: first-gift thank-you personalisation, lapsed reactivation, legacy intender nurture, mid-value donor development.
  • Grants and fundraising admin: application drafting support, restricted-fund tracking, funder reporting assembly, pipeline management.
  • Programme operations: case-management triage, service demand forecasting, safeguarding-aware signal detection.
  • Compliance and governance: Charity Commission reporting assembly, Fundraising Regulator evidence, UK GDPR posture monitoring.
  • Communications and campaigns: segment-aware content, email and social drafting with tone guardrails, campaign performance monitoring.

Pick one. Pilot on one team. Extend only if the return is real.

faq.question: Should a small charity even invest in AI?
faq.answer: Not always. We will say so if an engagement would not pay back. When we recommend it, the starting point is almost always fundraising operations or grants admin — where the time saved goes directly back to mission.

faq.question: How do you handle beneficiary data and safeguarding?
faq.answer: Beneficiary data gets treated with the same care as patient data in healthcare — minimisation, strict access controls, auditability, and safeguarding-aware design. We do not use beneficiary data to train models unless there is a very specific mission-aligned reason and proper consent.

faq.question: What systems do you integrate with?
faq.answer: The common charity stack — Raiser's Edge, Salesforce NPSP, Blackbaud, ThankQ, Donorfy — plus the case-management and grants platforms the sector uses. Integration effort is usually the binding constraint.

faq.question: Can AI help with grant applications?
faq.answer: Yes — as a drafting support for applications, compliance evidence for live grants, and reporting assembly at the end. The grants team still owns the funder relationship and the narrative; the agent takes the admin drag off.

faq.question: How do you price for the charity sector?
faq.answer: Every engagement is fixed-fee and scoped honestly. We want charities to get a real return — we will not stretch a project beyond where the value is clear, and we are open about what is and is not worth doing.

faq.question: How quickly does a charity see impact?
faq.answer: Fundraising operations and grants admin move fastest — inside a quarter. Programme-level impact takes longer and depends heavily on data quality and safeguarding posture.
